title: Sumo Logic keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Sumo Logic Access ID detected in source code. Sumo Logic Access IDs are 14-character alphanumeric identifiers that, when combined with an Access Key, provide API authentication to the Sumo Logic platform. These credentials grant access to: collect and query log data across your infrastructure, manage users and access controls within your Sumo Logic organization, create and modify dashboards, alerts, and scheduled searches, access sensitive security and compliance data stored in Sumo Logic, export data and search results programmatically, modify data collection and retention policies, access audit logs and system configuration data, integrate with third-party tools and services, and potentially access production monitoring and SIEM data containing sensitive business information. Exposing Access ID/Key credentials allows attackers to: query and exfiltrate sensitive log data including authentication logs, application secrets accidentally logged, PII/PHI data in application logs, security incident data and threat intelligence, financial transaction logs and audit trails, intellectual property in debug logs, internal API endpoints and infrastructure details discovered through log analysis, and customer data inadvertently captured in logs; manipulate monitoring and alerting by disabling critical security alerts, creating false alerts to cause alert fatigue, modifying alert conditions to hide malicious activity, deleting or tampering with audit logs, and disrupting SOC operations and incident response; consume resources and incur costs through excessive API queries consuming your query quota, running expensive searches across large time ranges, creating numerous dashboards and scheduled searches, triggering data re-indexing operations, and potentially causing thousands in overage charges for high-volume deployments; and gain intelligence about your security posture by analyzing what you monitor and what you don't, identifying gaps in security coverage, understanding incident response procedures through alert configurations, and mapping your infrastructure through log source analysis. Sumo Logic credentials also provide access to compliance and regulatory data which can expose: HIPAA/PHI data in healthcare application logs, PCI DSS cardholder data in payment processing logs, GDPR personal data across application telemetry, SOX financial audit trails and transaction logs, and other regulated data captured during normal operations. IMMEDIATELY revoke the compromised Access ID in Sumo Logic Administration (Administration → Security → Access Keys → Revoke), generate new Access ID/Key pairs with principle of least privilege following role-based access control, audit all API activity from the compromised credentials checking for unauthorized queries, data exports, configuration changes, user modifications, and suspicious access patterns, review recent searches and data access logs for potential data exfiltration, verify alert configurations haven't been modified to disable security monitoring, check for unauthorized dashboard creation or sharing, ensure no new users were created or permissions elevated by the compromised credentials, validate data collection and retention settings weren't changed, and investigate any unusual API usage spikes or patterns. Store Sumo Logic credentials securely using: environment variables (SUMO_ACCESS_ID, SUMO_ACCESS_KEY) never in code, secrets management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager), encrypted configuration stored outside version control, Kubernetes secrets with RBAC restrictions, or dedicated credential management for monitoring tools. Implement security best practices: create separate Access Keys for different services/teams with minimum necessary permissions, use service-specific roles limiting access to required log sources and actions only, enable IP allowlisting restricting API access to known IP ranges, rotate Access Keys regularly (every 90 days recommended), implement monitoring for unusual API patterns (queries outside business hours, large data exports, configuration changes, excessive failed authentication attempts, API calls from unexpected geography), maintain audit logs of all Access Key creation/modification/revocation events, use multi-factor authentication for administrative access to Sumo Logic, implement data masking for sensitive fields in logs at ingestion time, set up data access policies to restrict PII/PHI exposure, enable audit logging for all data access and configuration changes, configure query limits and throttling to prevent resource abuse, use Sumo Logic's built-in security analytics to detect compromised credentials, implement log retention policies balancing compliance needs with exposure risk, encrypt data in transit and at rest, regularly review and prune unused Access Keys and service accounts, document Access Key ownership and purpose for all active credentials, and implement break-glass procedures for emergency Access Key revocation. For enterprise deployments: consider using SAML SSO instead of Access Keys where possible, implement centralized secrets rotation with automated API key updates, deploy monitoring for Access Key usage across all environments, use separate Sumo Logic organizations for production vs non-production to limit blast radius, enable SIEM correlation rules to detect credential misuse across platforms, maintain incident response playbooks specifically for secrets exposure, and conduct regular access reviews ensuring Access Keys follow principle of least privilege.

Sumo Logic Access Key detected in source code. Sumo Logic Access Keys are 64-character base64-encoded secrets that, in combination with an Access ID, provide authenticated API access to the Sumo Logic cloud-based log management and analytics platform. These credentials grant comprehensive access to: collect logs and metrics from your entire infrastructure including servers, containers, cloud services, applications, network devices, and security tools; query and analyze all stored log data across your organization potentially containing PII, PHI, financial data, authentication tokens, API keys, passwords accidentally logged, intellectual property in debug logs, and sensitive business information; manage organizational settings including user access, roles, permissions, data retention policies, and security configurations; create, modify, and delete dashboards, alerts, scheduled searches, and automation rules; export large volumes of log data and search results programmatically; access audit logs revealing security incidents, user activities, and compliance events; integrate with SIEM, SOAR, and incident response platforms; manipulate data ingestion and parsing rules potentially hiding malicious activity; and access real-time security monitoring and threat intelligence data. Exposing Access Keys enables attackers to: exfiltrate sensitive data by running queries across all accessible log partitions searching for credentials, tokens, PII/PHI, financial data, exporting audit logs to understand security posture, downloading compliance reports and regulatory data, accessing application debug logs containing business logic, querying authentication logs to map user accounts, extracting network flow data to map infrastructure, and retrieving security alerts to understand threat detection capabilities; disrupt operations by deleting critical alerts causing missed security incidents, modifying alert thresholds to trigger alert fatigue, disabling log collection from key sources, corrupting dashboards used for business operations, manipulating scheduled searches and reports, exhausting query limits preventing legitimate monitoring, causing denial of service through resource-intensive queries, and tampering with audit logs to hide malicious activity; incur significant costs by running expensive queries across large time ranges and data volumes, creating numerous unnecessary scheduled searches consuming compute resources, triggering data re-indexing operations, exceeding ingest volume causing overage charges potentially worth thousands per month, and establishing long-running real-time queries consuming resources continuously; gain intelligence about security posture by analyzing what logs you collect revealing infrastructure and applications, what you monitor and what you don't exposing blind spots, alert configurations showing incident response procedures, retention policies indicating compliance requirements, user roles and permissions revealing organizational structure, integration points showing third-party tools and services, and patterns revealing deployment practices and change management procedures; and potentially access regulated data including HIPAA protected health information in medical application logs, PCI DSS cardholder data in payment processing systems, GDPR personal data across application telemetry, SOX financial audit trails, FERPA education records, and other compliance-related data captured during normal operations. For enterprises with extensive Sumo Logic deployments, compromised credentials can result in: data breaches exposing customer PII, financial data, or intellectual property leading to regulatory fines, reputational damage, and customer churn; compliance violations from unauthorized access to regulated data audit trails resulting in penalties potentially millions of dollars for HIPAA, PCI DSS, GDPR, or SOX violations; operational disruption from disabled alerts missing critical security incidents or system failures that could have been prevented; financial losses from excessive API usage charges, incident response costs, forensic investigation expenses, legal fees, and potential ransomware if attackers gain enough intelligence; loss of security visibility if attackers manipulate monitoring to hide ongoing attacks leading to prolonged dwell time and greater damage; and loss of trust in monitoring data integrity affecting SOC operations, incident response, and executive decision-making. IMMEDIATELY revoke the compromised Access Key in Sumo Logic (Administration → Security → Access Keys → Select and Revoke), generate new Access ID/Key pairs with minimum required privileges using role-based access control, conduct immediate forensic investigation of all API activity from the compromised credentials checking for: unauthorized queries showing potential data exfiltration attempts, unusual query patterns or timing indicating automated scanning, large data exports or downloads, configuration changes to alerts, dashboards, users, or permissions, new user accounts or permission escalations, disabled security alerts or monitoring, modified retention policies or data collection rules, suspicious scheduled searches or automation rules, and API calls from unexpected geographic locations or IP addresses. Review and secure all data accessed checking: recent query history for sensitive data searches, export logs for data exfiltration, alert modification history, user and role changes, dashboard access and sharing events, scheduled search execution logs, partition and data tier access patterns, and any anomalous access to compliance or audit data. Store new Sumo Logic credentials securely using: environment variables (SUMO_ACCESS_ID, SUMO_ACCESS_KEY) never committed to version control, secrets management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager) with encryption at rest and in transit, encrypted configuration files stored outside repositories deployed at runtime only, Kubernetes secrets with namespace isolation and RBAC controls, or dedicated secrets management in CI/CD pipelines with audit logging. Implement defense in depth: create dedicated Access Keys per service/application/environment with minimum necessary permissions scoped to specific log sources and actions, use service roles instead of admin credentials limiting permissions to required operations only (read-only for dashboards, write for ingestion, etc.), enable IP allowlisting restricting API access to known corporate networks, data center IP ranges, or specific service IPs, implement network segmentation ensuring Sumo Logic credentials only exist in production networks not accessible from development, rotate Access Keys on a regular schedule (every 90 days recommended or sooner for high-privilege keys), maintain detailed inventory of all Access Keys documenting ownership, purpose, permissions, creation date, and last rotation, implement comprehensive monitoring for: API calls outside normal business hours, queries accessing sensitive partitions or compliance data, large result set downloads or exports, configuration changes (alerts, users, permissions, retention), failed authentication attempts, API calls from unexpected geographic locations, unusual query patterns or frequencies, and changes to critical dashboards or searches. Use Sumo Logic's built-in security features: enable audit logging for all API access, user actions, and configuration changes, configure search audit to track all query activity, implement data access policies using partitions and role-based access control to restrict PII/PHI visibility, use field extraction rules to mask sensitive data at ingestion preventing exposure, enable real-time alerts for suspicious API activity or credential misuse, leverage Sumo Logic's security analytics to detect anomalous queries or access patterns, implement query quotas and rate limiting to prevent abuse, and regularly audit user access ensuring principle of least privilege. For enterprise deployments: consider using SAML/SSO for interactive access instead of Access Keys where possible, implement centralized secrets management with automated rotation updating all consuming services, maintain separate Sumo Logic organizations or deployments for production vs non-production environments limiting blast radius, use Sumo Logic's Cloud SIEM for advanced threat detection including credential compromise indicators, implement CSPM policies to detect overly permissive Access Keys, maintain incident response runbooks specifically for access key compromise scenarios, conduct regular access reviews pruning unused or overprivileged keys, implement data loss prevention monitoring for large exports or unusual query patterns, encrypt data at rest and in transit with customer-managed keys where applicable, and use Sumo Logic's integration with SIEM/SOAR platforms for automated threat response. Additionally: implement query content inspection for data exfiltration attempts, maintain query history baseline for anomaly detection, use Sumo Logic's AWS CloudTrail integration to correlate credential use across platforms, implement alert deduplication and enrichment to prevent alert manipulation going unnoticed, regularly test Access Key revocation procedures, document all Access Keys with business justification and risk assessment, and conduct red team exercises simulating credential compromise to validate detection and response capabilities.
